content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance/Administration . . . Executive/Legal . . . Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441

May 7, 1976

Mr. E. R. Skelly
2121 Arroyo Drive
Riverside CA 92506

Dear Mr. Skelly:

This letter will serve to confirm the conversation we had in my office on Wednesday, April 28, 1976. You indicated to me that you had reviewed the Chairman's response to Opinion Request No. 76-043, and you were troubled by the interpretation therein. In that letter, the Chairman indicated that, pursuant to the Commission's regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (a)(1)(D), an agency which makes "substantive recommendations which are, and over an extended period of time have been, regularly approved without significant amendment or modification by another ... governmental agency" is not an agency with a solely advisory function. To the contrary, such an agency possesses decision-making authority, and its members, therefore, are "public officials" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act.

You suggested that despite this standard, you believe that the Airport Commission functioned in a solely advisory capacity. You then proceeded to explain the manner in which the Airport Commission operates. As I understand it, a potential lessee will appear before the Commission and provide its members with a general idea of what the terms of a lease would be. In other words, the Commission will be given the rental amount and the basic terms, but will not be provided with an actual copy of the proposed lease. Based on this information, the Commission will then either approve or deny the request to enter into a lease. If the Commission approves the lease, it is turned over to the Airport Director and the City Attorney to negotiate the specific terms. If the negotiations are successful, the lease is signed by the lessee and then presented to the City Council for approval. As a practical matter, by the time the lease reaches the city council it usually is approved, and, in fact, the matter is often on the council's consent calendar and not even debated.


[illegible]

E. R. Skelly
May 7, 1976
Page Two

Based on this information, I stated that it seemed likely
the recommendations of the Airport Commission generally are
approved and, therefore, the regulation of the PPPC is apposite.
You then proceeded to explain why this may not be the case.
First, you pointed out that on at least one occasion, and
possibly others, the City Council had entered into a lease
without seeking the recommendations of the Airport Commission.1/
You also stated that even if the Airport Commission approves a
lease arrangement, the negotiations between the Airport Director
and the City Attorney and the potential lessee may not be
successful, and no lease would be presented to the council for
approval. Furthermore, you indicated that if the Airport
Commission were to approve a bad lease arrangement, the City
Attorney and/or the Airport Director could, and undoubtedly
would, recommend to the City Council that the lease not be
consummated. You suggested that under these circumstances,
the City Council would arbitrate the matter but may not be
willing to sign a lease. Finally, you pointed out the nature
of the lease presented to the City Council after negotiations
with the potential lessee can be very different than the gen-
eralized lease which was presented to the Airport Commission
for approval.

Based on this information, I indicated to you that your
position that the Commission is solely advisory is at least
tenable. I stated, however, that I would need more facts
before it would be possible to provide conclusive advice. In
particular, it would depend on whether, in light of all the
facts, the Airport Commission's recommendations were "regularly
approved without significant amendment or modification". I
suggested that in order to get a definitive resolution of the
questions, it would be best to submit an opinion request to
the PPPC outlining the relevant facts.

You indicated that you would discuss the matter with the
City Attorney and asked that I commit our discussion to writing.
I hope the foregoing accurately reflects the substance of that
discussion and, again, I apologize for the delay in forwarding
this letter to you. If you have any further questions, please
do not hesitate to call me.

Very truly yours,

Kenneth Pinney
Chief
Legal Division

KF:pvp

1/     This fact is more relevant to whether PPPC regulation 2
Cal. Adm. Code §18700(a)(1)(C), which provides that a body has
decision-making authority when its recommendation is a legal
prerequisite to a final governmental decision, is applicable.
Your example indicates that this subsection is not applicable
to the Airport Commission.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Airport Commission functions as a solely advisory body or possesses decision-making authority under FPPC regulations, specifically regarding its lease recommendations and their relationship to City Council approval.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC legal staff indicated that the Airport Commission's advisory status is at least tenable based on the facts presented, but a definitive answer requires more information about whether the Commission's recommendations are regularly approved without significant amendment or modification, suggesting an opinion request be submitted to the FPPC.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: ocr, old; LLM: OCR/old format document. This is FPPC outgoing correspondence documenting a prior conversation rather than a formal advice letter with standard Q/C sections. The document discusses interpretation of FPPC regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(1)(D) regarding advisory vs. decision-making functions. Staff indicates more facts are needed and recommends submitting an opinion request for definitive resolution.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC legal staff indicated that the Airport Commission's advisory status is at least tenable based on the facts presented, but a definitive answer requires more information about whether the Commission's recommendations are regularly approved without significant amendment or modification, suggesting an opinion request be submitted to the FPPC.

embedding.summary: This is a follow-up letter from FPPC Legal Division Chief Kenneth F. documenting a conversation with Mr. E.R. Skelly regarding whether the Airport Commission operates as a solely advisory body or has decision-making authority under FPPC regulations and the Political Reform Act.
